result:
I was thinking this morning about how quietly the future arrives. No fanfare, just a slow folding-over of days until one afternoon you find yourself speaking to a machine that answers back with something suspiciously like wit. I remember the first time I typed a question into a search bar and actually got the answer I needed; it felt like leaning on a wall and discovering it was a door. Now the doors are everywhere, disguised as pockets, lampposts, grocery aisles. We swipe them open without breaking stride.

I keep wondering what happens to longing when the interval between “I wish” and “here it is” collapses to a heartbeat. When I was twelve I saved for an entire summer to buy a second-hand Sony Walkman. I can still taste the plastic and the metal of its little body, still feel the embossed “Sony” under my thumb like a tiny monument to my own patience. The day it arrived—snail mail, two weeks late—was a personal public holiday. Now I tell an orbiting warehouse I want noise in my ears and it shows up before the calendar has time to feel guilty. Convenience is a kind of anesthesia; we’re all walking around with the ghosts of old cravings dangling off us like broken cobwebs.

There’s a walnut tree outside the window where I sit to write. Every autumn it rehearses the same magic trick: green clusters to chartreuse husks to the slow reveal of seam-split shells, wrinkled as wisdom. I’ve watched this performance nineteen seasons and I still can’t catch the moment of transformation. Meanwhile my phone can capture a million moments per second and still miss the only one that matters—the instant when something ordinary becomes irreplaceable. The brain is slower but choosier; it flags the scene where your grandfather lifts a cookie to his tea and the light lands just so on the rim of his glasses, and forty years later that single frame keeps screening itself in the cinema behind your eyes.

I’m not afraid of technology; I’m afraid of forgetting the texture of waiting. Delay used to be the kiln where anticipation hardened into story. Now the same stories arrive pre-fired, delivered in gleaming two-day-shipped boxes, and we unwrap them so fast we scorch our fingers. We’re left holding the artifact but skipping the archaeology of desire.

Tonight I turned the screen off early and walked to the corner store for nothing in particular. The air carried a rumor of rain, a metallic edge that made the streetlights feel sharpened. A stray cat followed me two blocks, tail high as a question mark. I had no notifications to check, no podcasts queued, so I listened to the soft percussion of my own shoes and the low hum of the city breathing through its thousand open windows. I felt, briefly, like an analog visitor inside my own digital life.

Maybe that’s enough: small insurgencies against the instant. Choosing the slow grocery line. Writing a letter. Waiting for the walnut tree. Letting the cat decide how far our fellowship extends. Somewhere in those pockets of imposed delay, the future still remembers how to knock instead of just walking right in.
